texts.orig: Abstract. In this paper we study the modules M every simple subfactors of which is a homomorphic image of M and call them co-Kasch modules. These modules are dual to Kasch modules M every simple subfactors of which can be embedded in M . We show that a module is co-Kasch if and only if every simple module in σ [ M ] is a homomorphic image of M . In particular, a projective right module P is co-Kasch if and only if P is a generator for σ [ P ] . If R is right max and right H -ring, then every right R -module is co-Kasch; and the converse is true for the rings whose simple right modules have locally artinian injective hulls. For a right artinian ring R , we prove that: (1) every finitely generated right R -module is co-Kasch if and only if every right R -module is a co-Kasch module if and only if R is a right H -ring; and (2) every finitely generated projective right R -module is co-Kasch if and only if the Cartan matrix of R is a diagonal matrix. For a Pr¨ ufer domain R , we prove that, every nonzero ideal of R is co-Kasch if and only if R is Dedekind. The structure of Z -modules that are co-Kasch is completely characterized.

texts.orig: A ring R is said to be right Kasch if every simple right R -module embeds in R . Commutative artinian rings and Quasi-Frobenious rings are well-known examples of Kasch rings. Kasch rings were generalized to modules by several authors. For example, in [1], a right R -module M is called a right Kasch module if every simple subfactor of M can be embedded into M . By a subfactor of M , we mean a submodule of a factor module of M .

texts.orig: Recall that, a ring R is said to be a right H -ring if the injective hulls of nonisomorphic simple right R -modules are homologically independent, that is, Hom R ( E ( S 1 ) , E ( S 2 )) = 0 for each nonisomorphic simple right R -modules S 1 and S 2 (see, [18]). Commutative Noetherian rings, and commutative semiartinian rings are H -rings (see, [8]). Thus, commutative perfect rings are H -rings, as left perfect rings are right semiartinian. Right artinian rings that are right H -ring are characterized in [19]. A ring R is said to be right max-ring if every nonzero right R -module has a maximal submodule.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, a notion dual to Kasch modules is proposed and studied. A natural dualization of Kasch modules is obtained as follows. We call a right module M right coKasch module if every simple subfactor of M is a homomorphic image of M. Semisimple and free modules are trivial examples of co-Kasch modules. More generally, for each right R -module M , the module M ⊕ R is a right co-Kasch module.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In section 2, we give some examples, closure properties and some characterizations of co-Kasch modules. We show that, M is a right co-Kasch module if and only if every simple S in σ [ M ] is a homomorphic image of M . A projective right R -module is co-Kasch if and only if P is a generator for σ [ P ] . We prove that, arbitrary direct sum of co-Kasch modules is again co-Kasch. On the other hand, direct summands or homomorphic image of co-Kasch modules need not be co-Kasch. It is shown that, if M is a co-Kasch right module, then M K is co-Kasch for each K ⊆ Rad( M ) .
texts.text: In section 2, we give some examples, closure properties and some characterizations of co-Kasch modules. We show that, M is a right co-Kasch module if and only if every simple S in σ [ M ] is a homomorphic image of M . A projective right R -module is co-Kasch if and only if P is a generator for σ [ P ] . We prove that, arbitrary direct sum of co-Kasch modules is again co-Kasch. On the other hand, direct summands or homomorphic image of co-Kasch modules need not be co-Kasch. It is shown that, if M is a co-Kasch right module, then M K is co-Kasch for each K ⊆ Rad( M ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In section 3, mainly we focus on investigation of the rings whose right R -modules are co-Kasch. Over a right V -ring, every right module is co-Kasch. If R is a local ring, then every right R -module is co-Kasch if and only if R is right perfect. We prove that, all right R -modules are co-Kasch if R is right H -ring and right max-ring; and the converse statement is true if the injective hulls of simple right R -modules are locally artinian. If R is right noetherian, then every right R -module is co-Kasch if and only if R is right artinian right H -ring. Note that a right R -module M is locally artinian if every finitely generated submodule of M is artinian; and R is right H -ring if there is no nonzero homomorphism between the injective hulls of nonisomorphic simple right R -modules.
texts.text: In section 3, mainly we focus on investigation of the rings whose right R -modules are co-Kasch. Over a right V -ring, every right module is co-Kasch. If R is a local ring, then every right R -module is co-Kasch if and only if R is right perfect. We prove that, all right R -modules are co-Kasch if R is right H -ring and right max-ring; and the converse statement is true if the injective hulls of simple right R -modules are locally artinian. If R is right noetherian, then every right R -module is co-Kasch if and only if R is right artinian right H -ring. Note that a right R -module M is locally artinian if every finitely generated submodule of M is artinian; and R is right H -ring if there is no nonzero homomorphism between the injective hulls of nonisomorphic simple right R -modules.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We also deal with rings whose cyclic or finitely generated right modules are co-Kasch. Over a commutative ring every cyclic module is co-Kasch. If R is either local or right H -ring, then every finitely generated right R -module is co-Kasch. Over a right artinian ring R , all right modules are right co-Kasch if and only if every finitely generated right R -module is co-Kasch if and only if Ext R ( U, V ) = 0 for each nonisomorphic simple right R -modules U and V. For a right artinian ring R, we prove that every finitely generated projective right R -module is co-Kasch if and only if the Cartan matrix of R is a diagonal matrix. If E ( R ) is projective, then E ( R ) is right co-Kasch if and only if R is right self-injective. For a commutative Noetherian ring R, we obtain that E ( R ) is co-Kasch if and only if R is a Kasch ring.

If E ( R ) is projective, then E ( R ) is right co-Kasch if and only if R is right self-injective. texts.label: text
texts.orig: If M is a co-Kasch right R -module which is either right max-projective or a right semiartinian, then M is retractable. We obtain a characterization of right H -rings in terms of right Kasch modules. We prove that, R is a right H -ring if and only if E ( S ) is a right Kasch module for each simple right R -module S . The rings whose right modules are Kasch are not completely known. Some sufficient conditions for such rings are given in [5]. We give a necessary condition for such rings. Namely, we show that if every right R -module is right Kasch, then R is a right H -ring and right semiartinian.

We obtain a characterization of right H -rings in terms of right Kasch modules. We prove that, R is a right H -ring if and only if E ( S ) is a right Kasch module for each simple right R -module S . The rings whose right modules are Kasch are not completely known. Some sufficient conditions for such rings are given in [5]. We give a necessary condition for such rings. Namely, we show that if every right R -module is right Kasch, then R is a right H -ring and right semiartinian.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In section 4, co-Kasch modules are studied over commutative rings. For a Pr¨ ufer domain R , we prove that R is Dedekind if and only if every nonzero ideal of R is co-Kasch. A characterization of co-Kasch modules over the ring of integers is obtained. Namely, we prove that, a nonzero torsion Z -module M is a co-Kasch module if and only if pM = M for each prime p with T p ( M ) = 0, where T p ( M ) is the p -primary component of the torsion submodule of M . If M = T ( M ), then M is a co-Kasch module if and only if pM = M for each prime p .

A characterization of co-Kasch modules over the ring of integers is obtained. Namely, we prove that, a nonzero torsion Z -module M is a co-Kasch module if and only if pM = M for each prime p with T p ( M ) = 0, where T p ( M ) is the p -primary component of the torsion submodule of M . If M = T ( M ), then M is a co-Kasch module if and only if pM = M for each prime p .

texts.orig: Lemma 2.4. Every simple module in σ [ M ] is isomorphic to a subfactor of M.
texts.text: Lemma 2.4. Every simple module in σ [ M ] is isomorphic to a subfactor of M.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let S be a simple module in σ [ M ] . Then there is a module N such that S ⊆ N and an epimorphism f : M ( I ) → N for some index set I. Let S = aR , where a ∈ N. Then a = f ( b ) for some b ∈ M ( I ) . Since b ∈ M ( F ) for some finite subset F of I, S ⊆ f ( M ( F ) ) . Therefore we can replace N by f ( M ( F ) ). So we have an epimorphism f : M 1 ⊕ . . . ⊕ M n → N for some positive integer n , where each M i = M and S ⊆ N. By induction on n we will prove that S is a subfactor of some M i , that is a subfactor of M. The case n = 1 is trivial. Let n = k > 1, and assume that the statement is true for all n < k. Denote M 2 ⊕ . . . ⊕ M n by L. If f ( M 1 ) ∩ S = 0 , then S ⊆ f ( M 1 ), therefore S is a subfactor of M 1 . If f ( M 1 ) ∩ S = 0 , then for the canonical epimorphism σ : N → N f ( M 1 ) = A we have Ker ( σ ) ∩ S = f ( M 1 ) ∩ S = 0 , so S ∼ = ( σf )( S ) ⊆ A. On the other hand, for the projection p : M 1 ⊕ L → L, we have Ker ( p ) = M 1 ⊆ Ker ( σf ) , therefore by Factor Theorem there is a homomorphism g : L → A such that σf = gp. Since gp = σf is an epimorphism g is an epimorphism as well. So we have an epimorphism g : M 2 ⊕ . . . ⊕ M n → A and S is isomorphic to a submodule of A . By the assumption of induction S is a subfactor of M i for some i = 2 , . . . , n, that is a subfactor of M. /square

texts.orig: Proposition 2.8. Let M be right co-Kash module. Then M K is co-Kasch for every K ⊆ Rad( M ) .
texts.text: Proposition 2.8. Let M be right co-Kash module. Then M K is co-Kasch for every K ⊆ Rad( M ) .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let S be a simple subfactor of M K . Clearly, S is a simple subfactor of M , as well. Then Hom R ( M,S ) = 0 , so there is a nonzero epimorphism f : M → S. Since S is a simple module, Ker( f ) is a maximal submodule of M . Thus Rad( M ) ⊆ Ker( f ) and so, by factor theorem, there is a nonzero homomorphism g : M K → S such that gπ = f, where π : M → M K is the canonical epimorphism. Therefore Hom R ( M K , S ) = 0 , and so M K is a co-Kasch module. /square

texts.orig: A ring R is said to be right co-noetherian if the injective hull of each simple right R -module is artinian. Right V -rings and QF -rings are trivial examples of right co-noetherian rings. Commutative noetherian rings are co-noetherian (see [17]). A ring R is said to satisfy the ( /diamondmath ) property if E ( S ) is locally artinian for each simple right R -module S. Right co-noetherian and right artinian rings satisfy ( /diamondmath ) . For characterizations of certain rings and algebras that satisfy the ( /diamondmath ) property we refer to [6], [9], [10], [14].

texts.orig: A nonzero idempotent e ∈ R is said to be primitive if eR is indecomposable as a right R -module (see, [16, Proposition 21.8]). Such modules eR are called principal indecomposable modules. Principal indecomposable right modules are local, and so each principal indecomposable module eR has a unique maximal submodule which is eJ, where J denotes the Jacobson radical of R. Every right artinian ring can be written as a direct sum of principal indecomposable right R -modules.

Every right artinian ring can be written as a direct sum of principal indecomposable right R -modules.

texts.orig: Proposition 3.9. If R is commutative, then every cyclic R -module is a co-Kasch module.
texts.text: Proposition 3.9. If R is commutative, then every cyclic R -module is a co-Kasch module.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let C ∼ = R I be a cyclic R -module and X be a simple subfactor of R I . Then X ∼ = A J ⊆ R J for some ideal J with I ⊆ J . Let P = ann R ( A J ). Note that Hom R ( R I , R P ) = 0 . Then, as X is a simple R -module, P is a maximal ideal of R with J ⊆ P. Then A J ∼ = R P and so A J is isomorphic to a simple factor of R I . Hence R I is a co-Kasch module. /square

texts.orig: Proposition 3.10. If R is a right H -ring, then every finitely generated right R -module is a co-Kasch module.
texts.text: Proposition 3.10. If R is a right H -ring, then every finitely generated right R -module is a co-Kasch module.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Suppose that R is a right H -ring, and M a finitely generated right R -module. Let S = A B ⊆ M B be a simple subfactor of M . There is a nonzero homomorphism f : M B → E ( S ). Then M Ker( f ) is isomorphic to a nonzero submodule of E ( S ). Since M Ker( f ) is finitely generated and nonzero, there is a maximal submodule K of M such that M K ∼ = U , where U is a simple subfactor of E ( S ). By the hypothesis, R is a right H -ring, thus U ∼ = S. Therefore every simple subfactor of M is isomorphic to a simple factor of M . Hence M is a co-Kasch module. /square
texts.text: Proof. Suppose that R is a right H -ring, and M a finitely generated right R -module. Let S = A B ⊆ M B be a simple subfactor of M . There is a nonzero homomorphism f : M B → E ( S ). texts.orig: Lemma 3.15. Let M be a right max-projective module. If M is a co-Kasch module, then M is retractable.
texts.text: Lemma 3.15. Let M be a right max-projective module. If M is a co-Kasch module, then M is retractable.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Let N be a nonzero submodule of M , and 0 = x ∈ N . Let K be a maximal submodule of xR . Since M is a co-Kasch module and xR K is a simple subfactor of M, Hom R ( M, xR K ) = 0. Let f : M → xR K be a nonzero homomorphism. By the max-projectivity assumption on M , there is a homomorphism g : M → xR such that πg = f , where π : xR → xR K is the canonical epimorphism. Then gi : M → N is a nonzero homomorphism, where i : xR → N is the inclusion map. Therefore, M is a retractable right R -module, as desired. /square

Then gi : M → N is a nonzero homomorphism, where i : xR → N is the inclusion map. Therefore, M is a retractable right R -module, as desired. /square

texts.orig: Proposition 3.18. If every right R -module is a Kasch module, then R is a right H -ring and right semiartinian.
texts.text: Proposition 3.18. If every right R -module is a Kasch module, then R is a right H -ring and right semiartinian.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Proof. Suppose every right R -module is Kasch. Then R is a right H -ring by Theorem 3.17. Let M be a nonzero right R -module and 0 = m ∈ M. Then mR has a maximal submodule K , and mR K is a simple subfactor of M . Therefore mR K embeds in M , because M is a Kasch module and so Soc( M ) = 0 . Thus the assumption implies that every nonzero right R -module contains a simple submodule. Hence R is right semiartinian.

Then mR has a maximal submodule K , and mR K is a simple subfactor of M . Therefore mR K embeds in M , because M is a Kasch module and so Soc( M ) = 0 . Thus the assumption implies that every nonzero right R -module contains a simple submodule. Hence R is right semiartinian.

texts.orig: The converse of Proposition 3.18 is not true in general. That is, there are right semiartinian right H -rings that admit right modules that are not right Kasch. For example the ring given in [1, Remarks 3.14 (3)], is a semartinian V-ring (hence an H-ring) which has a module that is not Kasch.

Now consider the ideal I = ∩ n ∈ Z + Rp n . Then by [2, Proposition 15] I has no maximal submodules i.e. Rad( I ) = I . Thus, since I is co-Kasch by the assumption, I = 0, by Proposition 3.1(1). Therefore R is a DVR by [4, page 99, Exercise 4]. /square

For the proof, we need to show that Hom( I, R J ) = 0 for each maximal ideal J of R . Assume that Hom( I, R J ) = 0 for some maximal ideal J of R . Then I = J · I . texts.text: ⇒ (1) Let I be a nonzero ideal of R. Note that I is finitely generated. Since R embeds in I , I has a subfactor isomorpic to every simple R -module. For the proof, we need to show that Hom( I, R J ) = 0 for each maximal ideal J of R . Assume that Hom( I, R J ) = 0 for some maximal ideal J of R . Then I = J · I . Localizing at the maximal ideal J , we obtain I J = J J · I J . Since R J is a local ring with the unique maximal ideal J J ,the fact that I J is finitely generated and I J = J J · I J implies that I J = 0 by Nakayama's Lemma. As R is a domain I J = 0 gives I = 0 . Contradiction. Therefore I = J · I for each maximal ideal J of R , and so Hom( I, R J ) = 0. Hence I is a co-Kasch module, and this proves (1) . /square

If p is a prime integer the submodule T p ( M ) = { m ∈ M | p n m = 0 for some positive integer n } is said to be the p -primary component of M . It is well known that T ( M ) = ⊕ p ∈P T p ( M ), where P is the set of prime integers.

texts.orig: (a) A nonzero torsion Z -module M is a co-Kasch module if and only if pM = M for each prime p with T p ( M ) = 0 .
texts.text: (a) A nonzero torsion Z -module M is a co-Kasch module if and only if pM = M for each prime p with T p ( M ) = 0 .

texts.orig: (b) If T ( M ) = M , then M is a co-Kasch module if and only if pM = M for each prime p .
texts.text: (b) If T ( M ) = M , then M is a co-Kasch module if and only if pM = M for each prime p .

texts.orig: Proof. ( a ) Since M is torsion, M = ⊕ p ∈ A T p ( M ), where T p ( M ) is the (nonzero) p -primary component of M . Suppose M is an H -module. Let p be a prime such that T p ( M ) = 0. Then S = Soc( T p ( M )) ∼ = Z p Z is a subfactor of M . Since M is a co-Kasch module, M L ∼ = Z p Z . This implies that pM = M . This show the necessity. Conversely, let X Y ∼ = Z p Z be a simple subfactor of M . We have X Y ⊆ M Y ∼ = ⊕ T p ( M ) T p ( Y ) . For a prime q = p , Hom( Z p Z , T q ( M ) T q ( Y ) ) = 0. Therefore, Hom( Z p Z , T p ( M ) T p ( Y ) ) = 0 . So that T p ( M ) = 0. Now by the hypothesis pM = M , and so M has a simple factor isomorphic to Z p Z .
